subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated and focused, providing a solid investigative path
The topic is introduced early with a concise title and key terms (mitigation strategies, PM10/PM2.5, Katowice vs WaÅbrzych) that effectively communicate purpose
Overall structure of the introduction and focus on twoâcity comparison engages the reader
weaknesses: The scope (two cities over eight years) is broad, occasionally diffusing focus
Methodology is underdeveloped â lacks operational definitions, justification of data sources and discussion of reliability
Some background sections (global context, Paris Agreement) are loosely linked to the local research question
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work communicates the topic adequately and formulates a clear research question, but the broad scope and limited methodological detail prevent it reaching the top band. Strand assessments placed topic clarity in the 3â4 band, the research question in the 3â4 band and methodology in the 1â2 band. A mark of 4/6 reflects solid communication and focus, tempered by an underdeveloped methods section and overly broad framing.

strengths: Use of key geographical terminology (e.g. PM10, mitigation, scale, process) is generally accurate
Relevant factual details and official statistics from municipal reports demonstrate subject knowledge
Links to IB concepts such as scale and power are explicit
weaknesses: Occasional imprecision (misâlabelling of units, conflation of pollutants with greenhouse gases)
Superficial treatment of physical processes (dispersion, meteorology)
Limited engagement with academic literature and weak critical synthesis of sources
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strandsâuse of terminology and demonstration of subject knowledgeâfall into the midâband (3â4) with some lapses in precision and limited depth of source critique. A score of 3/6 accurately reflects clear but not comprehensive knowledge and occasional conceptual imprecision.

strengths: The argument follows a logical comparative structure and consistently refers back to the research question
Secondary data is applied with clear relevance to mitigation measures in each city
Analysis includes basic causeâandâeffect links (e.g. bus fleet renewal and NOâ reduction)
weaknesses: Overâreliance on descriptive narrative, with limited triangulation or critique of data reliability
Conclusions are often correlational rather than rigorously evaluated (no statistical testing)
Minor inconsistencies and unsupported generalisations weaken coherence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each strand of critical thinking (research application, analysis and evaluation, argument coherence) sits at the lower end of the 4â6 band. The work applies relevant data and develops an argument but remains largely descriptive with intermittent critical evaluation. A mark of 4/12 reflects this adequate but not consistently strong engagement with evidence and reasoning.

strengths: Clear table of contents, numbered headings and logical sectioning support reader navigation
Figures and tables are present and generally labelled
Title page and word count provided
weaknesses: Page numbering is absent in the body of the report
An empty âMethodologyâ subsection and misplacement of evaluation in the contents page disrupt flow
Inconsistent figure captions and occasional informal language hinder professional presentation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The reportâs structure is coherent and mostly aids understanding, but layout conventions are only partially applied. Inconsistent labelling, missing page numbers and tone issues keep it in the midâband (3â4). A score of 3/4 captures an overall organised presentation with room for refinement.

strandSelections.reasoning: The investigation draws on relevant secondary datasets (air-quality monitoring, municipal reports) and applies them to the research question with reasonable consistency.  Data for both cities over nine years are tabulated and graphed, and linked to specific policy actions.  However, the use of sources is essentially descriptive; there is little triangulation, no primary data, and limited critique of reliability.  Application is thus âadequate with some relevanceâ rather than consistently strong, placing it at the lower end of the 4-6 bandâscore 4.

The student names data sources (e.g., GIOÅ air-quality database), outlines comparison of pollutant trends, and lists mitigation actions for each city.  However, the âMethodologyâ subsection is extremely brief and largely empty; it neither justifies indicator selection nor explains data reliability, sampling, or analytical techniques beyond simple percentage change calculations.  There is no discussion of alternative sources, limitations of secondary data, or treatment of confounding variables (e.g., meteorology).  Consequently, the methodology is present but limited and uneven, aligning with band 1-2; a top-of-band score of 2 is appropriate.

The essay demonstrates a sound grasp of key geographical ideasâindustrialisation, mitigation, scale, processes, powerâand links them to the Polish context.  The student supplies relevant factual detail on pollution levels, bus fleets and heating replacements, drawing on official statistics and municipal reports.  Nevertheless, treatment of physical processes (e.g., dispersion, meteorological influences) is superficial, and there is limited engagement with academic literature on urban air-quality management.  Source use is mostly descriptive; citations are provided but critical synthesis is weak.  This results in generally clear but not comprehensive knowledge, consistent with the mid-band (3-4) and a score of 3.

Basic IB layout requirements are met: title page with word count, tables and figures labelled, and page numbers indicated in the contents table.  However, page numbering inside the document is absent, some figure captions are duplicated or misplaced, and font/spacing choices occasionally hinder readability.  Citations are present but inconsistent in style.  These issues show that layout conventions are only partly applied, fitting the 1-2 band; a score of 2 is therefore appropriate.

Core geographical terms (e.g., PM10, mitigation, reforestation, EURO 6, smog, greenhouse gases) are employed correctly in most cases, and the student explicitly references IB Geography concepts such as scale, process and power.  Occasional imprecision (mis-labelling of units, conflation of âpollutantsâ with âgreenhouse gasesâ, confusion over ozone data) shows that conceptual control is not always sustained.  Nonetheless, the terminology is more accurate than not, fitting the 3-4 band; a mid-band mark of 3 reflects the intermittent lapses in precision.

The topicâ the effect of mitigation strategies on air-pollution trends in two Polish citiesâis introduced early, the title is explicit and the introductory paragraph restates it clearly. Key terms (mitigation strategies, PM10/PM2.5, Katowice vs WaÅbrzych) are visible throughout, allowing the reader to understand what is being investigated. However, some phrasing is wordy and the dual-city comparison plus an eight-year time frame make the scope broader than ideal, occasionally diffusing the focus. Overall clarity is adequate rather than fully precise, placing the work in the 3-4 band; a mark of 3 best represents the moderate but not consistently sharp communication of purpose.

The essay follows a logical comparative structureâIntroduction, Analysis, Conclusionâso the line of argument is generally traceable.  Evidence from tables and graphs is used to support claims, and the conclusion explicitly answers the research question.  Nonetheless, the argument sometimes drifts into narrative description; critical engagement with counter-viewpoints or alternative explanations is thin, leading to occasional over-generalisation.  Minor inconsistencies (e.g., contradictory total-pollutant figures) further weaken coherence.  These features meet the descriptor for an âeffective with some minor inconsistenciesâ argument at the lower end of the 4-6 band, warranting a score of 4.

The student calculates percentage reductions and juxtaposes pollutant trends with mitigation timelines, demonstrating an attempt at analysis.  Basic cause-and-effect links are drawn (e.g., bus fleet renewal â lower NOâ), and limitations are briefly acknowledged.  Yet much of the discussion remains correlational; external variables (meteorology, economic change) are merely noted, not explored quantitatively, and some conclusions (e.g., âstrategies were successfulâ) are stated without rigorous statistical testing.  Evaluation is intermittent, aligning with âadequate with partially supported conclusionsâ; thus a band 4-6 with a score of 4 is justified.

A clear table of contents, numbered headings and sub-headings, and separate sections for introduction, analysis, conclusion, and evaluation provide an organised framework that aids reader navigation.  While generally logical, some elements are misplaced (e.g., an empty âMethodologyâ sub-section, evaluation preceding references on the contents page) and figure/table labelling is occasionally inconsistent.  These shortcomings prevent a fully seamless structure, but overall it supports understanding, meriting the upper band (3-4) with a score of 3.

The research question is stated verbatim in the abstract and again in section 1.1: âTo what extent has air pollution in Katowice in 2015-2023 decreased â¦ and how can it be compared to WaÅbrzych?â  The wording identifies pollutant reduction and spatial comparison, showing a reasonable investigative path.  Nevertheless, the phrasing merges two objectives (magnitude of change and comparison) without specifying which indicators or methods will operationalise âto what extentâ, leaving partial ambiguity about measurement and evaluation criteria.  Clarity and focus are therefore solid but not exemplary, meriting a band 3-4 and a score of 4.

annotations.comment: The statement that WaÅbrzychâs strategies âwere not taken until 2025â contradicts the 2015â2023 scope; revise to maintain chronological accuracy.
The comparative quantification of heating system replacements is clear and directly tied to emission reductions, supporting the investigationâs analytical depth.
The table of contents lists sections clearly but misaligns with actual page numbering and omits the empty methodology subsection; revise to match the final document structure and pagination.
The use of first-person (âI am a citizenâ¦â) diminishes academic tone. Rephrase in the third person to maintain formal report style.
This section remains largely descriptive; the student should integrate analytical commentary and interpret the significance of pollutant trends in light of strategies.
The section systematically identifies and compares transport mitigation strategies in both cities, demonstrating thorough application of secondary research.
Table structure is clear but the numbering (âTable 1â) and caption placement are inconsistent; apply IB formatting conventions for tables to improve clarity.

The research question merges two objectives without operational definitions for âto what extentâ; the student should specify the indicators and methods to be used for measuring pollutant reduction and comparison.
The reforestation discussion lacks equivalent quantitative data for Katowice; include planting figures to enable a balanced comparison of green strategies.
The infographic conveys key data but is overcrowded and text sizes vary; simplify graphics and improve spacing for better readability.
The phrase âTransportation was helpfulâ is colloquial; adopt precise academic language (e.g. âTransport measures led toâ¦â) for clarity and formality.
The title is concise and clearly communicates the investigationâs focus on mitigation strategies and air pollution comparison, providing a strong foundation for reader engagement and clarity.
The student-created map is clear and well-labelled, effectively situating Katowice and WaÅbrzych spatially and enhancing the geographical context of the study.
The introduction covers two cities over eight years, resulting in a very broad scope. Narrow the focus or include sub-questions to sharpen the investigationâs boundaries.
The discussion of IB concepts is largely descriptive and lacks depth in physical process analysis; incorporate scholarly sources to deepen conceptual application.
The global climate change context is informative but only loosely connected to the two-city analysis. Tie this section more directly to local air-quality drivers.
Decimal separators are inconsistent (commas vs. periods) and units are not LaTeX-formatted; standardize to e.g. $$\mu\text{g}/\text{m}^3$$ throughout.
The overview of the Paris Agreement adds general background but lacks explicit linkage to municipal mitigation strategies; clarify its relevance to the research question.
The historical industrial context is detailed yet uncited. Include primary or secondary source references to validate historical population and mining data.
Several causal links (e.g. buses â NOâ reduction) are asserted without controlling for confounding factors; consider additional quantitative analysis to support these claims.
The table caption splits across blocks and is unnumbered; merge caption text and ensure correct placement immediately below the table.
Bus procurement figures are comprehensive but uncited; include specific source citations for all municipal data to strengthen evidential credibility.
The line graph is well-labelled but omits error bars or confidence intervals; include measures of variability to strengthen data interpretation.
Bar chart colors have low contrast; select a palette that ensures accessibility and clear differentiation between bars.
The conclusion summarizes findings effectively but omits mention of study limitations; integrating reflection on data constraints would enhance critical evaluation.
Figure scales vary between graphs, hindering direct comparison; adopt a consistent axis range for total pollutants across figures.
The evaluation section uses the first person (âmy studyâ); rephrase to third-person when reflecting on methodology to maintain formal academic tone.
Works cited entries show inconsistent punctuation and formatting; apply a uniform citation style and include hanging indents for clarity.
Several online sources lack access dates; include retrieval dates for web references to meet IB formatting requirements.
